verses.text: At that time Abijah the son of Jeroboam fell sick. And Jeroboam said to his wife, Arise, I pray thee, and disguise thyself, that thou be not known to be the wife of Jeroboam; and get thee to Shiloh: behold, there is Ahijah the prophet, which told me that I should be king over this people. And take with thee ten loaves, and cracknels, and a cruse of honey, and go to him: he shall tell thee what shall become of the child. And Jeroboam's wife did so, and arose, and went to Shiloh, and came to the house of Ahijah. But Ahijah could not see; for his eyes were set by reason of his age. Guidance: A dying child, a desperate king, a disguised queen. Abijah — Jeroboam's son, named with the LORD's name (Aviyyah — 'my father is the LORD' — an ironic name in this idolatrous house) — falls dangerously ill. Jeroboam, who has rejected the LORD's worship, now turns secretly to the LORD's prophet for help. The Hebrew detail is telling: he wants to consult Ahijah the Shilonite — the same prophet who had originally torn the cloak into twelve pieces and given him ten tribes (1 Kgs 11:29-39). Jeroboam knows that Ahijah's word is the LORD's word. But he is too compromised to come himself, and too ashamed to send his queen openly. The disguise is the chapter's framing detail. The wife (unnamed) is to dress like a commoner, bring a commoner's small gift (ten loaves, cracknels — possibly small dotted cakes — and a cruse of honey, the standard offering from a peasant woman to a holy man), and ask anonymously. Jeroboam is hoping the disguise will let him hear good news from the prophet untainted by association with the apostate kingship. The chapter is preserving a sad truth: the same king who built the golden calves still believes the LORD's prophet has real knowledge of his son's fate. He has not lost faith in the LORD's God; he has only lost the courage to face Him directly. 'But Ahijah could not see; for his eyes were set by reason of his age.' The detail is important. Ahijah is now blind. The disguise should work — a blind prophet cannot recognize a disguised face. But the LORD's prophet sees by a different vision than the eyes.

And the LORD said unto Ahijah, Behold, the wife of Jeroboam cometh to ask a thing of thee for her son; for he is sick: thus and thus shalt thou say unto her: for it shall be, when she cometh in, that she shall feign herself to be another woman. And it was so, when Ahijah heard the sound of her feet, as she came in at the door, that he said, Come in, thou wife of Jeroboam; why feignest thou thyself to be another? for I am sent to thee with heavy tidings. Guidance: The LORD's preemptive briefing. Before the queen arrives, the LORD tells Ahijah exactly who is coming, why she has come, and what to say. The disguise is irrelevant; the LORD has already told His prophet the truth. When Ahijah hears 'the sound of her feet' at the door, he calls out: 'Come in, thou wife of Jeroboam; why feignest thou thyself to be another?' The chapter is teaching us a fundamental truth about prophetic insight. The LORD's prophet does not see through disguises by ordinary perception; the LORD tells him what to see. Human cleverness in concealment is no match for divine revelation. Compare 1 Sam 16:7 — 'man looketh on the outward appearance, but the LORD looketh on the heart.' The blind Ahijah, by the LORD's word, sees more clearly than the disguising queen. 'I am sent to thee with heavy tidings.' Hebrew anokhi shaluach eilayikh qashah — 'I am sent to thee with hardness.' The verdict is severe; the chapter is preparing the queen (and the reader) for what is about to come. The chapter is showing us that the LORD does not allow His prophets to be deceived by political theater; nor does He let the questioner walk away with a comforting half-truth. The disguise has not protected Jeroboam from the LORD's word; if anything, it has marked him as a man unwilling to face the LORD directly even in family crisis.

Go, tell Jeroboam, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Forasmuch as I exalted thee from among the people, and made thee prince over my people Israel, And rent the kingdom away from the house of David, and gave it thee: and yet thou hast not been as my servant David, who kept my commandments, and who followed me with all his heart, to do that only which was right in mine eyes; But hast done evil above all that were before thee: for thou hast gone and made thee other gods, and molten images, to provoke me to anger, and hast cast me behind thy back: Therefore, behold, I will bring evil upon the house of Jeroboam, and will cut off from Jeroboam him that pisseth against the wall, and him that is shut up and left in Israel, and will take away the remnant of the house of Jeroboam, as a man taketh away dung, till it be all gone. Him that dieth of Jeroboam in the city shall the dogs eat; and him that dieth in the field shall the fowls of the air eat: for the LORD hath spoken it. Guidance: The terrible message. The chapter records one of the harshest prophetic indictments in the Old Testament. The structure: (1) Recital of the LORD's gift. 'I exalted thee from among the people, and made thee prince over my people Israel, and rent the kingdom away from the house of David, and gave it thee.' Jeroboam owes everything to the LORD's choice; he was no royal heir, just an Ephraimite labor-supervisor whom the LORD elevated. (2) The contrast with David. 'Yet thou hast not been as my servant David, who kept my commandments, and who followed me with all his heart.' David's standard, again the benchmark. (3) The specific indictment: 'Thou hast done evil above all that were before thee: for thou hast gone and made thee other gods, and molten images, to provoke me to anger, and hast cast me behind thy back.' Three offenses: making other gods (the golden calves), making cast-metal images, and 'casting me behind thy back' (Hebrew hishlakhta acharei gavekha — the standard idiom for treating something as worthless waste-matter to be thrown over one's shoulder). The LORD whose gift Jeroboam received has been treated as garbage. (4) The verdict: 'I will bring evil upon the house of Jeroboam, and will cut off from Jeroboam him that pisseth against the wall' — the crude Hebrew idiom (mashtin ba-qir) refers to males, comprehensively. The whole male line will be wiped out. 'I will take away the remnant of the house of Jeroboam, as a man taketh away dung, till it be all gone.' The image of dung-removal is deliberately humiliating; Jeroboam treated the LORD as worth throwing away, so the LORD will treat his dynasty as the same. (5) The unburied corpses: 'Him that dieth of Jeroboam in the city shall the dogs eat; and him that dieth in the field shall the fowls of the air eat.' Denial of burial — the deepest dishonor an Israelite could suffer (compare 2 Sam 21 — Rizpah's vigil to prevent it). The chapter is recording the strongest possible curse on a king's house. The chapter is teaching us that those who treat the LORD as disposable will find their own legacies treated with the same finality.

Arise thou therefore, get thee to thine own house: and when thy feet enter into the city, the child shall die. And all Israel shall mourn for him, and bury him: for he only of Jeroboam shall come to the grave, because in him there is found some good thing toward the LORD God of Israel in the house of Jeroboam. Guidance: The merciful exception. Abijah, the sick prince, will die — that is the immediate verdict the queen has come to hear. But within the catastrophe is a small mercy. 'When thy feet enter into the city, the child shall die.' The Hebrew is precise: the moment the queen's feet cross the city threshold, the child will die. The LORD has timed it precisely. Why this exception in mercy? 'For he only of Jeroboam shall come to the grave, because in him there is found some good thing toward the LORD God of Israel in the house of Jeroboam.' Hebrew niimtza-bo davar tov — 'there is found in him a good word/thing.' Abijah alone, in the apostate king's house, has shown some 'good thing toward the LORD God of Israel.' The text does not specify what — perhaps the young prince had quietly worshipped at Jerusalem, or shown sympathy to the Levites his father had displaced, or spoken against the calves. We don't know. What we know is that the LORD noticed. The chapter is showing us a profound principle. In the worst household, the LORD's eye still sees individual hearts. Abijah's brief life will end early, but he will receive what his entire family will not — honored burial with national mourning. The grave at the city's edge that no other Jeroboamite male will reach will be Abijah's. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD distinguishes between members of even the most apostate families. The mass-judgment on a household does not erase the individual recognition of any heart in it that turned toward Him. Abijah, dying young, escapes the worse fate of his dynastic survivors who will be eaten by dogs in the streets. The mercy is brutal — early death — but real.

Abijah, dying young, escapes the worse fate of his dynastic survivors who will be eaten by dogs in the streets. The mercy is brutal — early death — but real.

Moreover the LORD shall raise him up a king over Israel, who shall cut off the house of Jeroboam that day: but what? even now. For the LORD shall smite Israel, as a reed is shaken in the water, and he shall root up Israel out of this good land, which he gave to their fathers, and shall scatter them beyond the river, because they have made their groves, provoking the LORD to anger. And he shall give Israel up because of the sins of Jeroboam, who did sin, and who made Israel to sin. Guidance: The long-range prophecy. (1) A king will rise who cuts off Jeroboam's house — this is Baasha (1 Kgs 15:27-30), who within a generation will exterminate the Jeroboam line. (2) Then, much further out: 'The LORD shall smite Israel, as a reed is shaken in the water, and he shall root up Israel out of this good land… and shall scatter them beyond the river.' This is the Assyrian exile — fulfilled in 722 BC, about 200 years after Ahijah's words. The 'river' is the Euphrates; Israel's northern population would be deported across it to the Assyrian heartland (2 Kgs 17). The image is unforgettable: Israel like a marsh-reed shaking in the water — once-firm, now uprooted. The reed appears strong but lacks deep roots; pulled up easily by floodwaters. So Israel, despite its apparent vigor, lacked the deep covenant-roots that would have held it against the coming Assyrian flood. (3) The reason given: 'They have made their groves' — Asherah-poles, the wooden cult-pillars dedicated to the Phoenician goddess Asherah, set up at the high places. The idolatry that began with Jeroboam's calves had developed into the full Canaanite cult by the 8th century BC. (4) The summary indictment: 'because of the sins of Jeroboam, who did sin, and who made Israel to sin.' The formula chatatu Yarav'am asher chata va-asher he-chati et-Yisrael — Jeroboam's own sin and his causing-Israel-to-sin. From here forward in 1-2 Kings, this dual indictment will be applied to every northern king. The Hebrew distinction is important: not just Jeroboam's personal sin, but his structural sin of building institutions that compelled his subjects' apostasy. The chapter is teaching us a sobering principle about leadership. A leader's personal sin is bad enough; a leader who institutionalizes sin so that his successors and his people are caught in the system is responsible for sins he never personally committed. The destruction of the northern kingdom in 722 BC traces back, in the LORD's accounting, to Jeroboam's golden-calf decision two centuries earlier.

And Jeroboam's wife arose, and departed, and came to Tirzah: and when she came to the threshold of the door, the child died; And they buried him; and all Israel mourned for him, according to the word of the LORD, which he spake by the hand of his servant Ahijah the prophet. Guidance: The exact fulfillment. The queen returns home to Tirzah (the new northern capital — Jeroboam had moved from Shechem; Tirzah, a few miles northeast, would be the capital until Omri built Samaria around 880 BC). 'When she came to the threshold of the door, the child died.' Ahijah's word had specified 'when thy feet enter into the city'; the narrator narrows it further to 'the threshold of the door' (saph ha-bayit) — the precise moment her foot crossed the doorframe. The timing is exact to the inch. 'All Israel mourned for him' — a national mourning for the young prince in whom 'some good thing toward the LORD' had been found. The chapter is showing us prophecy fulfilled to the precise moment specified. The LORD's word does not approximate; it lands. The narrator's parenthesis — 'according to the word of the LORD, which he spake by the hand of his servant Ahijah the prophet' — is the explicit theological frame. The chapter is teaching us that biblical prophecy operates with a particular precision; when the LORD specifies timing, the timing holds. Compare the fulfillment of the man of God's altar-prophecy in chapter 13 (the altar split immediately) or the fulfillment of Ahijah's earlier cloak-prophecy in chapter 12 (Solomon's kingdom torn). The LORD's word is reliable down to the threshold-step.

The LORD's word is reliable down to the threshold-step.

And the rest of the acts of Jeroboam, how he warred, and how he reigned, behold, they are written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel. And the days which Jeroboam reigned were two and twenty years: and he slept with his fathers, and Nadab his son reigned in his stead. Guidance: Jeroboam's death summary. The standard regnal formula: cross-reference to the official annals ('the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel' — a separate source from the canonical 1 Kings, now lost), reign length, dignified-death formula, succession. He reigned 22 years (roughly 931-910 BC, by standard chronology). 'Nadab his son reigned in his stead.' Nadab will reign only two years before Baasha assassinates him and exterminates the Jeroboam house (1 Kgs 15:25-30), fulfilling the dung-removal prophecy of v10. The chapter is preserving the dry royal-record format that will define the rest of 1-2 Kings: each king's reign is introduced and concluded with a formal frame, with the theological evaluation embedded between. From this point forward the narrative alternates between northern and southern kings, with chronological synchronisms ('in the Nth year of king X of Y, king Z began to reign over W'). The chapter is teaching us that even kings whose dynasties are doomed get the formal honors in the annals. Jeroboam's reign is recorded as a real piece of national history; the LORD's verdict on him does not erase the historical record.

And Rehoboam the son of Solomon reigned in Judah. Rehoboam was forty and one years old when he began to reign, and he reigned seventeen years in Jerusalem, the city which the LORD did choose out of all the tribes of Israel, to put his name there. And his mother's name was Naamah an Ammonitess. Guidance: The chapter pivots south to Rehoboam in Judah. The introductory formula gives us his age (41 at accession), reign length (17 years), capital (Jerusalem), and crucially his mother (Naamah the Ammonitess). The mother's identity matters because of the deuteronomic warning about foreign wives and their religious influence. Naamah was Ammonite — from the same nation whose national god Molech demanded child sacrifice. She was probably one of Solomon's seven hundred wives mentioned in 1 Kgs 11. Her influence on her son's religious choices is implied by the chapter's juxtaposition. 'Jerusalem, the city which the LORD did choose' — the deuteronomic name-theology emphasis. Even as Judah's religious life would drift, the LORD's specific choice of Jerusalem as the place of His name remains the chapter's note. The chapter is showing us the structural difference between the northern and southern kingdoms: Judah retains the temple, the chosen city, the Davidic dynasty. These are the LORD's gifts to Judah even when Judah's kings are bad. The chapter is teaching us that institutional grace can outlast personal failure. The temple Solomon built will stand for almost four more centuries despite the corruption of many Davidic kings; the LORD's choice of Jerusalem will preserve a remnant through every catastrophe. The southern kingdom will fall (in 586 BC) but not as quickly as the northern, and not without preserved hope for restoration. The chapter is preparing the long parallel-narrative that lies ahead.

And Judah did evil in the sight of the LORD, and they provoked him to jealousy with their sins which they had committed, above all that their fathers had done. For they also built them high places, and images, and groves, on every high hill, and under every green tree. And there were also sodomites in the land: and they did according to all the abominations of the nations which the LORD cast out before the children of Israel. Guidance: Judah's evil under Rehoboam. The chapter is honest about Judah's failure even though Judah retains the temple. 'Judah did evil in the sight of the LORD, and they provoked him to jealousy with their sins which they had committed, above all that their fathers had done.' The Hebrew vayqannehu — they provoked Him to jealousy. The jealousy-language is covenant-marriage language; the LORD is Judah's covenant-husband, and Judah is sleeping with other gods. Three specific institutional sins: (1) High places (bamot) on every hill — local altars outside the temple system, in violation of Deut 12's single-sanctuary law. (2) Images (matzevot) — stone pillars associated with Canaanite worship. (3) Groves (asherim) — Asherah-poles, wooden cult-pillars dedicated to the goddess. Plus the broader category: 'sodomites in the land' (qadeshim — male cult-prostitutes associated with Canaanite fertility worship, not 'sodomy' in the modern sexual-orientation sense but the technical category of male temple-prostitutes whose function was ritual sex with worshippers). 'They did according to all the abominations of the nations which the LORD cast out before the children of Israel.' Judah is becoming the very thing the conquest was meant to remove. The chapter is showing us that having the temple does not automatically produce faithful worship. The temple stood in Jerusalem; the Davidic king sat on the throne; the priests served at the altar. But the population was also building local high places, erecting Asherah-poles, and supporting male cult-prostitutes. The institutional infrastructure was correct; the practical religion was syncretistic. The chapter is teaching us a hard truth about religious institutions: orthodoxy of place and structure does not guarantee orthodoxy of practice. A people can have the right temple and still drift into false worship through accumulation of small compromises.

And it came to pass in the fifth year of king Rehoboam, that Shishak king of Egypt came up against Jerusalem: And he took away the treasures of the house of the LORD, and the treasures of the king's house; he even took away all: and he took away all the shields of gold which Solomon had made. Guidance: Shishak's invasion. In Rehoboam's fifth year (about 925 BC by standard chronology), Pharaoh Shishak (Sheshonq I of the Twenty-second Dynasty) invaded the Levant. Egyptian records confirm this campaign — the Bubastite Portal at the temple of Karnak in Egypt preserves Shishak's victory inscription listing the cities he subdued, including many in both Israel and Judah. The biblical and Egyptian records align. Shishak — the same Pharaoh who had earlier sheltered Jeroboam (1 Kgs 11:40) — now invades. (The alliance between Jeroboam and Shishak likely encouraged the campaign; Shishak was using northern intelligence against southern Judah.) The Egyptian army reached Jerusalem, and Rehoboam paid the standard ancient surrender-tribute: 'he took away the treasures of the house of the LORD, and the treasures of the king's house; he even took away all.' Solomon's accumulated wealth — the unweighed bronze of 1 Kgs 7, the gold of 1 Kgs 10 — flowed south to Egypt. The chapter is preserving the specific detail: 'he took away all the shields of gold which Solomon had made.' The 500 ceremonial gold shields of 1 Kgs 10:16-17 — the dazzling display-arms of the House of the Forest of Lebanon — are stripped and carted to Egypt. The chapter is showing us a precise theological connection. Solomon accumulated gold by violating the deuteronomic prohibition on multiplying wealth (Deut 17:17); Solomon's son loses that gold to the same Egypt the deuteronomic law had warned the king never to return to (Deut 17:16). The gold flowed south to Egypt — the precise reversal of the Exodus, in which gold had flowed north out of Egypt to Israel. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's discipline often involves stripping away what was acquired in disobedience. The shining symbols of Solomon's glory are now in a Pharaoh's treasury.

(The alliance between Jeroboam and Shishak likely encouraged the campaign; Shishak was using northern intelligence against southern Judah.) The Egyptian army reached Jerusalem, and Rehoboam paid the standard ancient surrender-tribute: 'he took away the treasures of the house of the LORD, and the treasures of the king's house; he even took away all.' Solomon's accumulated wealth — the unweighed bronze of 1 Kgs 7, the gold of 1 Kgs 10 — flowed south to Egypt. The chapter is showing us a precise theological connection. Solomon accumulated gold by violating the deuteronomic prohibition on multiplying wealth (Deut 17:17); Solomon's son loses that gold to the same Egypt the deuteronomic law had warned the king never to return to (Deut 17:16). The gold flowed south to Egypt — the precise reversal of the Exodus, in which gold had flowed north out of Egypt to Israel. And king Rehoboam made in their stead brasen shields, and committed them unto the hands of the chief of the guard, which kept the door of the king's house. And it was so, when the king went into the house of the LORD, that the guard bare them, and brought them back into the guard chamber. Guidance: The bronze replacement — the chapter's most poignant symbolic detail. Unable to replace the stolen gold, Rehoboam makes bronze shields instead — a substantially cheaper, less prestigious metal — and entrusts them to the royal guard. The bronze shields function as the new ceremonial-display arms when the king visits the temple. They look superficially similar; they serve the same function; they are made by skilled craftsmen. But everyone knows they are not gold. The Hebrew nechoshet (bronze) replacing zahav (gold) is the visible measure of the dynasty's diminishment. The chapter's detail: 'when the king went into the house of the LORD, that the guard bare them.' The royal procession from the palace to the temple — the same procession the queen of Sheba had marveled at in 1 Kgs 10:5 — still happens. The visual is preserved. But the substance has been swapped. Solomon's gold shields traveled the path in dazzling sun-flash; Rehoboam's bronze shields travel the same path in dimmer reflection. The chapter is preserving a metaphor that has resonated for centuries. The bronze-for-gold substitution is a small parable of every dynasty in decline: the forms continue, the substance has eroded. The procession still proceeds. The shields still march. But what was originally golden is now only bronze, and everyone old enough to remember knows. The chapter is teaching us that the spiritual cost of compromise often shows up not in dramatic collapse but in slow material substitution. The temple still stands; the king still processes; the shields still march. But the gold is gone, and the bronze will never recover what was lost.

Now the rest of the acts of Rehoboam, and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah? And there was war between Rehoboam and Jeroboam all their days. And Rehoboam slept with his fathers, and was buried with his fathers in the city of David. And his mother's name was Naamah an Ammonitess. And Abijam his son reigned in his stead. Guidance: Rehoboam's death summary. The standard regnal closing: cross-reference to 'the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah' (a separate annal from the canonical 1 Kings, now lost), the comment on the continued north-south hostility ('war between Rehoboam and Jeroboam all their days'), the standard burial formula, the mother's identity repeated for emphasis (Naamah the Ammonitess — the source of the foreign-religious influence Rehoboam grew up under), and the successor (Abijam, also called Abijah in 2 Chr 12). The chapter ends with Judah's transmission to its third generation: David → Solomon → Rehoboam → Abijam. The decline is steady; each generation has more accumulated compromise than the last. The chapter is preserving the parallel-record structure that 1-2 Kings will maintain for the next 200 years of dual kingdoms. The narrator alternates: a chapter on the northern king, a chapter on the southern king, both evaluated against the LORD's standard, both falling further as the centuries pass. The chapter is teaching us, in compact form, that history under the LORD's discipline does not move in steady-state. Either a king turns the people back to the LORD (rarely), or the slow drift downward continues. Rehoboam's reign is the latter — institutional Judaism continues, with bronze where gold used to be, with high places everywhere and male cult-prostitutes in the land, with the temple still standing as a witness against the very practices the population engaged in. The book of Kings will trace this slow descent down to its catastrophic ends in 722 BC (north) and 586 BC (south). The chapter ends the first phase of that descent: the two opening kings, both flawed, both buried, both succeeded by sons who will continue the pattern.

Now the rest of the acts of Rehoboam, and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah? And there was war between Rehoboam and Jeroboam all their days. And Rehoboam slept with his fathers, and was buried with his fathers in the city of David. And his mother's name was Naamah an Ammonitess. And Abijam his son reigned in his stead. Guidance: Rehoboam's death summary. The standard regnal closing: cross-reference to 'the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah' (a separate annal from the canonical 1 Kings, now lost), the comment on the continued north-south hostility ('war between Rehoboam and Jeroboam all their days'), the standard burial formula, the mother's identity repeated for emphasis (Naamah the Ammonitess — the source of the foreign-religious influence Rehoboam grew up under), and the successor (Abijam, also called Abijah in 2 Chr 12). The chapter ends with Judah's transmission to its third generation: David → Solomon → Rehoboam → Abijam. The decline is steady; each generation has more accumulated compromise than the last. The chapter is preserving the parallel-record structure that 1-2 Kings will maintain for the next 200 years of dual kingdoms. The narrator alternates: a chapter on the northern king, a chapter on the southern king, both evaluated against the LORD's standard, both falling further as the centuries pass. The chapter is teaching us, in compact form, that history under the LORD's discipline does not move in steady-state. Either a king turns the people back to the LORD (rarely), or the slow drift downward continues. Rehoboam's reign is the latter — institutional Judaism continues, with bronze where gold used to be, with high places everywhere and male cult-prostitutes in the land, with the temple still standing as a witness against the very practices the population engaged in. The book of Kings will trace this slow descent down to its catastrophic ends in 722 BC (north) and 586 BC (south). The chapter ends the first phase of that descent: the two opening kings, both flawed, both buried, both succeeded by sons who will continue the pattern.

Now the rest of the acts of Rehoboam, and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah? And there was war between Rehoboam and Jeroboam all their days. And Rehoboam slept with his fathers, and was buried with his fathers in the city of David. And his mother's name was Naamah an Ammonitess. And Abijam his son reigned in his stead.
